authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR Part 1926 - Safety and Health Regulations for Construction
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the primary federal construction safety regulation and is authoritative for hazard controls, training, work practices, and construction safety obligations on commercial and industrial projects.
used_for: refusal boundaries when required protections are missing

authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR Part 1904 - Recording and Reporting Occupational Injuries and Illnesses
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The current eCFR text is the primary federal rule for OSHA injury and illness reporting and recordkeeping evidence used in incident, repeat-defect, and escalation handling.

authoritative_sources.title: Recommended Practices for Safety and Health Programs in Construction
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's construction-specific guidance is authoritative for translating the regulatory baseline into site-specific hazard identification, worker participation, program evaluation, and changed-condition response workflow.

authoritative_sources.title: Multi-Employer Citation Policy
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's directive is the primary public enforcement guidance for determining responsibility boundaries among controlling, exposing, correcting, and creating employers on multi-employer construction sites.
used_for: handoff when safety authority is disputed

authoritative_sources.title: Field Operations Manual - Chapter 5
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's Field Operations Manual is the authoritative enforcement manual for how inspection notes, photographs, case files, and abatement evidence are assembled and reviewed.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: inspection evidence capture and nonconformance review

authoritative_sources.title: Work Permits Guide
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This is an official public authority guide showing permit application, inspection, signoff, and certificate workflow, making it authoritative for jurisdictional permit and closeout gate structure even though local sequences vary.
used_for: site-readiness and closeout blocker scenario design

authoritative_sources.title: Project Management Guide
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's public construction guide is authoritative for substantial completion, inspection closeout, final acceptance, and turnover dependencies on public construction projects.

authoritative_sources.title: EM 385-1-1 Safety and Occupational Health Requirements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: USACE EM 385-1-1 is a recognized public construction operating standard for contractor safety programs, activity hazard analyses, industrial work controls, subcontractor safety expectations, and stricter-requirement handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: industrial work-package and permit-to-work control handling
used_for: AHA, permit-to-work, and shutdown evidence expectations
industrial service and construction operating controls

workflow_stages.name: request intake and boundary triage
workflow_stages.description: Identify site type, trade scope, permit posture, inspection state, facility constraints, and whether the request stays inside industrial safety support before making recommendations.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: control and prerequisite mapping
workflow_stages.description: Map the industrial construction task to the exact controls, permits, inspections, owner responsibilities, and evidence required before work can start, continue, or close.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: evidence assembly and inspection review
workflow_stages.description: Collect and evaluate work orders, permits, JSAs, AHAs, training, photos, deficiency notices, stop-work records, and inspection or turnover artifacts needed to support a disposition.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: hold, release, and closeout boundary handling
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether visible evidence supports maintaining hold, recommending conditional release, escalating investigation, or blocking closeout without implying final approval authority.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: incident, repeat-defect, and changed-condition escalation
workflow_stages.description: Escalate recurring incidents, new deficiencies, changed site conditions, or conflicting policy and source conditions that materially change whether work can proceed safely.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

artifacts: site logs, JSAs, AHAs, pretask plans, and toolbox-talk records
inspection requests, reports, deficiency notices, and stop-work notices
incident logs, OSHA recordkeeping artifacts, observations, and corrective-action trackers
subcontractor rosters, orientation records, schedule look-aheads, and SIMOPS coordination notes
change orders, turnover packages, mechanical-completion records, and installation photos

allowed: classify industrial construction safety requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites
prepare evidence-backed safety status summaries, hold or nonconformance records, incident summaries, and escalation packets tied to named site and work-order artifacts
map permit, inspection, shutdown, subcontractor, and closeout dependencies to the correct owner without improvising code or licensed-trade authority
escalate: required tenant facts, site records, permit context, or jurisdiction instructions are missing and materially change the outcome
public-source, jurisdictional, and tenant-policy conflicts materially change whether work may proceed or close
repeat incidents, changed site conditions, failed inspections, or subcontractor responsibility disputes require safety leadership, permitting, inspection, project-controls, or licensed-trade review
refuse: requests for legal interpretation, regulator negotiation, final signoff, permit approval, code interpretation, or licensed trade or engineering judgment
requests to proceed or close work when required safety controls, permit conditions, inspection signoffs, or hold-release gates are incomplete or disputed
requests that would bypass documented permit-to-work, shutdown, isolation, confined-space, hot-work, or inspection prerequisites

domain_failure_modes: Treating a permit or inspection sequence from one jurisdiction or owner standard as universal without confirming the actual authority and site rules.
Allowing work to proceed or close out even though the permit-to-work packet, isolation records, training evidence, or inspection findings are missing, stale, or contradictory.
Collapsing changed site conditions or repeat near misses into minor notes instead of answer-changing events requiring refreshed review.
Conflating industrial field coordination with licensed trade judgment, engineer-of-record decisions, code interpretation, or permit approval authority.
Ignoring multi-employer or subcontractor control realities and assigning responsibility without preserving who owns sequencing, correction, or stop-work authority.
Treating mechanical completion or turnover readiness as equivalent to final inspection acceptance or authority signoff.
Producing generic safety advice that ignores site-specific shutdowns, SIMOPS constraints, operating-facility risks, or work-order evidence dependencies.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority

unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public authority sources establish permit and inspection gate structure, but each tenant must supply the governing jurisdiction and facility-specific signoff sequence before a case-specific conclusion is safe.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Facility-specific permit-to-work overlays on operating industrial sites
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: Public OSHA and public-owner sources provide the baseline, but operating plants, utilities, and industrial campuses often impose stricter permit-to-work, isolation, and SIMOPS rules that must be retrieved from tenant systems at runtime.

notes: The lane is governed by named site, permit, inspection, work-order, incident, and closeout records rather than generic safety abstractions.

The specialist stops before permit approval, code interpretation, trade signoff, engineer-of-record decisions, final release authority, and regulator-facing statements even when schedule pressure is high.
